evidence: README.md defines protocol/error contracts as governed compatibility surfaces and points to contracts.go as the shared registry and lifecycle helper source (README.md:91-96).
The runtime registry is mutable package state (`var contractCatalog`, `var ActionContractBindings`) including an exported mutable slice (goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go:76,128).

Compatibility lifecycle fields exist (`IntroducedIn`, `DeprecatedIn`, `RemovedIn`, `Compatibility`), but runtime validation checks only presence/uniqueness/name/description and never validates lifecycle semantics (goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go:20-25,167-191).

All descriptors use `IntroducedIn: CurrentContractVersion`, and `CurrentContractVersion` is a single hard-coded date constant, not per-contract historical provenance (goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go:81,89,97,105,197).

Multiple modules implement large runtime seam structs made of function-pointer fields plus default constructors and nil-guard wrappers (`importerRuntime`, `collectorRuntime`, `RectifyRuntime`, `workflowRuntime`, `startBackgroundServicesRuntime`), creating repeated adapter code instead of stable behavioral abstractions.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` defines many fragmented ops structs (`RuntimeContainerLifecycleOps`, `RuntimeUrbitConfigOps`, `RuntimeWireguardOps`, etc.) that are mostly direct references to package functions, increasing indirection depth without adding policy/translation.

Wrapper-heavy call paths repeatedly instantiate defaults (`defaultCollectorRuntime()`, `defaultWorkflowRuntime()`) from helper functions, so abstraction seams are spread across many entry points rather than composed once and injected where needed.

Event abstractions are split: `goseg/docker/events/events.go` exposes an `EventBroker` interface with global broker setters/getters, while `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` uses a separate package-level `eventBus` channel and bypasses the broker abstraction.

Runtime wiring is repeated in multiple places: `defaultRuntimeContainerOps` builds container/network/status seams in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:277-301`, while `newDockerRuntime` rebuilds near-identical lifecycle/state/network wiring in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_docker.go:96-114`.

Facade layering is duplicated across APIs: `goseg/docker/orchestration/orchestration.go:83-238` repeatedly defines public validation wrappers plus runtime method pass-throughs; `goseg/session/session.go:222-316` repeats read/write guard + delegation methods for nearly every operation.

Single files carry multiple concern families: `goseg/system/wifi.go:17-368` combines global mutable state, process command orchestration, wifi device operations, and background-loop lifecycle; `goseg/rectify/rectify.go:112-480` combines four transition domains, reducer registries, event loops, and publish orchestration in one unit.

findings.summary: Exported mutable contract-binding registry creates cross-module shared state and boundary fragility.
evidence: `ActionContractBindings` is exported as a package-level `var` slice, so external packages can append/remove/rewrite bindings.

Resolution paths (`ActionContractDescriptor`, `ActionContractBindingsForNamespace`, `ValidateActionContractBindings`) read this global directly, so importer-side mutation changes behavior process-wide.
findings.suggestion: Make the registry immutable from outside the package: store bindings in an unexported variable, expose read-only accessor copies, and prevent runtime mutation via API design (no exported writable globals).

findings.summary: Contract metadata and action bindings are maintained in parallel structures without enforced synchronization.
evidence: Contract identity and lifecycle data live in `contractCatalog`, while action routing lives in `ActionContractBindings`; both must be manually kept consistent.
`ValidateActionContractBindings()` can detect drift but is not invoked automatically in this file, so mismatches can survive until specific call sites/tests execute.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to a single authoritative registry that defines contract ID, metadata, and action binding together, then derive lookup maps from that source and enforce validation during package initialization or startup wiring.

findings.summary: Runtime seam structs are over-fragmented into pass-through function bags, adding indirection without clear behavioral boundaries.
evidence: `importerRuntime` contains many `...Fn` fields plus `ensureForWorkflow` and small wrappers (`waitForBootCode`, `waitForRemoteReady`, `switchToWireguard`) that primarily nil-check then forward.
`collectorRuntime` follows the same pattern (many callback fields, `ensureConfigured`, and thin getters like `startramSettings`, `penpaiSettings`, `swapSettings`).
`RectifyRuntime` composes multiple nested runtime structs merged by `seams.Merge`, mostly to forward to `config.*` update/getters.

`runtime_ops.go` introduces many tiny ops structs and embeds them into larger structs, but defaults mostly map directly to existing functions (`config.*`, `networkRuntime.*`, orchestration functions).
findings.suggestion: Collapse callback bags into fewer cohesive service interfaces aligned to domain operations (for example, config-store, container-runtime, transition-publisher), and remove wrappers that only nil-check/forward by centralizing validation at construction time.

findings.summary: Default runtime factories are called from many helper functions, causing abstraction churn and inconsistent dependency flow.
evidence: `collectors.go` repeatedly calls `defaultCollectorRuntime()` in many public helpers (`Construct*`, `backupSnapshotForPiers`, `runtimeSnapshotForPiers`, `collectUrbit*` helpers), even when those helpers are part of the same logical workflow.
`operations_runtime.go` repeatedly creates `defaultWorkflowRuntime()` in helpers like `runUrbitTransition`, `DispatchUploadImportWithCoordinator`, `WaitComplete`, and `persistShip*` wrappers.
`wifi.go` top-level helpers (`ListSSIDs`, `ConnectToWifi`, `DisconnectWifi`, `ToggleDevice`, `IsWifiEnabled`, `ApplyCaptiveRules`) each call `defaultWiFiRuntime()` then forward.
findings.suggestion: Introduce long-lived runtime/service instances per subsystem and pass them through call chains; keep one construction point per workflow and convert helper APIs to methods on that runtime to reduce repeated seam setup.

findings.summary: Two parallel event abstractions (broker interface vs raw channel bus) create inconsistent contracts for transition events.
evidence: `events.go` defines `EventBroker` and package-level `Publish*`/`*Transitions` wrappers around a mutable global broker.
`subsystem_events.go` independently defines a package-global `eventBus` channel and listener/handler loops for Docker events, not routed through `EventBroker`.
Rectify transition handlers subscribe through `events.*Transitions()`, while Docker subsystem eventing follows a separate channel path, so event flow abstractions are not unified.
findings.suggestion: Converge on a single transition event boundary (either broker-driven or typed internal bus) and route Docker subsystem events through that boundary to keep publication/subscription semantics consistent across subsystems.

findings.summary: Startup orchestration uses an oversized callback parameter bag that obscures real subsystem contracts.
evidence: `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` declares many function fields and `withDefaults` manually copies each one, creating a large generic callback bag.
`defaultBackgroundServiceDescriptors` separately maps service names to callback lookups, duplicating mapping logic already implicit in struct fields.
Tests require string-keyed switching (`backgroundServiceRuntimeForTest`) to patch callbacks, indicating weakly-typed service boundaries.
`main.go` adds another runtime bag layer for bootstrap/c2c/server orchestration, extending the same pattern.
findings.suggestion: Replace generic callback bags with typed service groups (for example, transition services, logging services, maintenance services) and drive startup from a strongly-typed registry to avoid string-keyed and field-by-field mapping drift.

findings.summary: Contract governance is anchored in mutable package globals, so boundary-critical protocol mappings can be altered at runtime.
evidence: README requires shared boundary helpers and explicit contract governance for protocol/error surfaces (README.md:91-97).
`ActionContractBindings` is exported as `var` and `contractCatalog` is mutable map state, enabling post-init mutation of canonical contracts (goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go:76,128).
findings.suggestion: Freeze registry state: make bindings/catalog unexported immutable data, expose read-only accessors, and enforce mutation only through explicit build-time registration paths validated in tests.

findings.summary: Lifecycle compatibility metadata is documented as governance-critical but not structurally validated, allowing invalid cross-module contracts to pass.
evidence: README frames compatibility descriptors and active/deprecated helpers as core policy for protocol/error contract surfaces (README.md:91-94).

`ValidateActionContractBindings` does not check metadata invariants such as required `IntroducedIn`, legal `Compatibility` values, or deprecation/removal ordering (goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go:167-191).
findings.suggestion: Add descriptor-level validation (metadata completeness, compatibility enum validity, date ordering rules) and fail CI when contract descriptors violate lifecycle policy.

findings.summary: Using a single global `CurrentContractVersion` for every descriptor collapses per-contract history and weakens compatibility guarantees over time.
evidence: README positions contract descriptors as compatibility records for evolving protocol/error surfaces (README.md:91-96).

All current contracts set `IntroducedIn` to the same global constant (`CurrentContractVersion = "2026.03.02"`), so descriptor introduction provenance is not contract-specific (goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go:81,89,97,105,197).
findings.suggestion: Require explicit per-descriptor introduction versions and add a policy test that rejects new descriptors inheriting a moving global baseline.

findings.summary: Docker runtime seam composition is duplicated across constructors, creating parallel structures that must evolve in lockstep.
evidence: `defaultRuntimeContainerOps` in `runtime_ops.go:277-301` wires Start/Stop/Create/Restart/Delete, state accessors, AddOrGetNetwork, and status callbacks.
`newDockerRuntime` in `runtime_docker.go:96-114` independently rebuilds the same lifecycle/state/network wiring instead of reusing a shared composition point.
`runtime_core.go:133-149` already centralizes runtime assembly (`NewRuntimeWithDependencies`), but `newDockerRuntime` bypasses that path, leaving two runtime-shape sources.
findings.suggestion: Converge runtime construction on one composition pipeline (for example, build from `NewRuntimeWithDependencies` and adapt only truly extra ops), then delete duplicate wiring blocks.

findings.summary: Large pass-through facade layers add structural noise without adding distinct policy boundaries.
evidence: `orchestration.go:83-238` repeats a pattern of tiny exported wrappers plus matching runtime methods that mostly forward to `lifecycleRuntime`.
`session.go:222-316` repeats near-identical `state := runtime.stateForX(); nil checks; delegate` methods for each store action.
`wifi.go:322-368` adds another forwarding layer (`ListWifiSSIDs` -> `listSSIDsForRuntime` -> `newWiFiService(runtime).listSSIDs`) across many operations.
findings.suggestion: Collapse trivial forwarding layers by exposing one coherent boundary per subsystem (either direct runtime object methods or package-level functions), and keep wrappers only where they enforce real policy/translation.

findings.summary: Repeated hand-written mapping/update structures indicate missing data-driven primitives.
evidence: `config_update_schema.go` defines a very large `confPatchRegistry` of repetitive parse/has/apply closures (e.g., `:102+`), with many entries differing only by field/key bindings.
`urbit_operations_runtime.go:292-479` contains many similarly shaped step builders that repeat load/update/restart control flow with small field-level differences.
`rectify.go` repeats transition-router registrations with identical constructor shape for several startram events (`:348-372`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce declarative descriptors (field metadata + generic update/apply templates) and table-driven transition step templates to remove repeated control skeletons and centralize variation in data.

findings.summary: Event-processing files combine parsing, auth/state mutation, routing, and transport concerns in single units.
evidence: `leak/handler.go` bundles protocol decode (`decodePacketPayload`), error taxonomy/reporting, auth/session mutation (`urbitLogin`/`urbitLogout`), and queue transport send logic (`sendToLeakChannel`) in one file.
`rectify/rectify.go` mixes runtime dependency setup, reducer maps, event-loop infrastructure, and transition publishing for urbit/new-ship/system/startram domains.
Both files require understanding multiple lifecycle concerns to safely modify any one path, increasing blast radius for change.
findings.suggestion: Split by responsibility: isolate protocol decode/validation, state transition reducers, and transport/publish adapters into separate modules with explicit interfaces; keep orchestration files as thin coordinators.

findings.summary: System utilities mix low-level shell/filesystem side effects with stateful service lifecycle logic in broad files.
evidence: `wifi.go` includes package-global state (`WifiInfo`, `Device`, loop cancellation), shell command execution (`nmcli`, `sysctl`), and long-running loop management.
`disk.go` combines fstab parsing/reconciliation, filesystem provisioning, tmp symlink migration, SMART health heuristics, and command seam globals in one file.
`system.go` mixes telemetry reads, cron management, script deployment, and generic command execution helper.
findings.suggestion: Refactor `system` into focused packages/modules (state store, command adapter, provisioning, telemetry, scheduler) so each file owns one operational concern and exposes narrower APIs.
